narrative:
A couple of rounds of showers are coming, one during the early afternoon. This will be followed by a break during the mid-to-late afternoon where fewer showers will be around, there might even be a short peek of sunshine. Then another batch of showers and thundershowers arrives this evening, ahead of a front that moves through tonight. Showers taper off later tonight and Thursday morning. Then cooler air arrives—Thursday night will be chilly. Friday is cool and dry, followed by a warmer and more pleasant Saturday. Clouds increase again Sunday with isolated showers developing in the afternoon from south to north and increasing during the evening before tapering off late Sunday night into Monday.

It's the May the Fourth Be With You edition of the Monday Message, now available to our subscribers.

2027 Blue Ridge Calendar Photo Contest

Voting in the 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar Photo Contest ended Sunday. We will announce the winners by the end of this week. Many thanks to everyone who submitted photos and voted in the contest. It's going to be another awesome calendar!

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Subscribe Tuesday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion A large, slow-moving trough moves into the eastern U.S. It weakens somewhat Saturday, only to be reinforced as a second surge of cooler air arrives by Monday. Our immediate issue is a cold front—the leading edge of this trough—pushing through tonight. This afternoon will see scattered showers ahead of the approaching front. We’ll have a break by mid-afternoon before a second round of showers and thundershowers develops late in the day, and continues into tonight. It’s much-needed rain after months of drought. Showers taper off Thursday morning behind the front and become limited to the western escarpment, ending there by Thursday afternoon. Much cooler air pushes in from the northwest Thursday into Thursday night, leading to a seasonally cool but dry Friday. After a cool start, Saturday turns warmer and more pleasant. Another front approaches Sunday and moves through Sunday night. Meanwhile, low pressure develops near the Southeast Coast and moves into the Mid-Atlantic by early Monday. Clouds return Sunday, with isolated showers developing in the afternoon from south to north. Expect steadier rain Sunday evening, tapering off later Sunday night into Monday morning. Temperatures turn noticeably cooler again Monday, then rebound somewhat Tuesday as the center of high pressure moves across the Southeast. Submitted by Angie Melcher. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights April 2026 Recap The Main Story They say that "April showers bring May flowers." If that is the case, what does April drought bring? Well, we may (pun intended) be about to find out, because April was about as dry as can be for many folks around here. We'll get further into those details later. By and large, it was a quiet month, characterized by long stretches of dry, warm weather with a handful of low temperatures exceeding the average high temperature for the date. Boone notched 16 highs of 70 degrees or more, in a month where the average high does not exceed 70 degrees at all (peaking with an average of 66F on the 30th). While most stayed dry or saw a brief shower on April 1st, the most notable moment of the month for Deep Gap unfolded as a prolific hail storm rumbled through the area. Locals reported noticeable hail accumulation, with largely pea to marble sized hail coating the ground in a few select spots. A "cooler" period marked the first third of the month, although it still featured multiple days at or above average. From the 10th on to the 18th... warm and dry. Downtown Asheville recorded its warmest temperature of 2026 to date on the 15th at 89F. There was no rain throughout this stretch of the month. The graph below tells the story, with the big swings of February and March a thing of the past: the coldest and warmest highs were 27 degrees apart, whereas in March they were 50 degrees apart (87F and 37F). Perhaps the most impressive thing about this chart is not the string of 80s mid-month, but the fact that there was not a single freeze. This marks the second consecutive April that the Asheville airport did not record a freeze, which broke a streak of 12 consecutive April "last freezes." In fact, dating back to 1873, only 15 last freezes have occurred in March. Barring a May freeze (which is not unheard of, certainly), we will move that number to 16. The only major cooldown of the month occurred around April 20th, when Beech Mountain residents reported brief flurries mixed in with a night of freezing fog and rime ice that briefly restored the winter feel. Mount Mitchell dropped to 24 degrees at noontime on the 19th, the second coldest daily low of the month. April went out with two beneficial rains, moving eastward across the area the mornings of the 28th and the 30th. Many locations totaled 0.5 to 1.5 inches of rain from those two events... the majority of April rains. Local Details

Warm, warm, warm. No other way to slice it, April was well above normal. In fact, it was the warmest April on record at six local climate sites: Boone, Beech Mountain, Hot Springs, Old Fort, Galax, and Hickory. Many other locations achieved top 5 or top 10 warmest Aprils. The region ran 4 to 8 degrees above normal. Highs of 91.4 on the 15th and 18th at the Morganton-Lenoir Airport were the warmest of any station in the Ray's Weather coverage area all month. Below is a map of temperature anomalies for the Contentintal U.S. for April.

Of course, as everyone is well aware: it is dry. April did not help this, with the majority of stations recieveing less than half of their usual April rainfall. North Wilkesboro wins the badge of honor (or shame) for least rain in the month of April in Western N.C. and Virginia. Just 0.66" for an entire month! Most of that came from the aforementioned morning rains during the final week of the month. Streams and rivers are running slow, and lakes and reservoirs are showing plenty of lakebed now. We've flipped into a more active pattern to start May, but we will need more help to dig out of the deficit, rain-wise. May 05, 2026 Monday Message for May 4, 2026 It's the May the Fourth Be With You edition of the Monday Message, available now to our subscribers! May 04, 2026 May 2026 Weather Outlook Monthly Forecast Forecast for the Southern Appalachians – SW Virginia & Western North Carolina The May Pattern: Transition Month May is a transition month across the Southern Appalachians. The trees become fully green even at the highest elevations, and bloom season is on full display. Frost potential goes almost to nil by the end of the month, even in the most frost-prone mountain valleys. Climatologically, it’s one of the wetter months of the year, often featuring a battle between lingering cool air masses and increasing warmth and humidity from the south. That clash can produce frequent showers and thunderstorms, along with some of the more active weather of the spring season. Bottom Line for May 2026 April ran warmer and much drier than average, but the end of April took a much cooler turn. That late-April cooldown marks the start of two weeks of cooler-than-average weather in May. But the last two weeks of May will be warmer than average, leading to a draw for the month overall. Expect significant variation, averaging out to near-normal for the month. For precipitation, we start with a relatively dry week, followed by near-average precipitation for the rest of the month, resulting in slightly drier-than-average conditions overall. Temperatures: Chilly Start, Warm Finish The extended-range guidance from the ECMWF, GFS, and Canadian models is in decent agreement on the overall temperature pattern. An eastern trough persists over the eastern United States, supporting cooler-than-average temperatures through mid-May. The transition to this pattern is already underway in the final days of April. In week three of May, the trough weakens and the pattern gradually transitions toward a more zonal flow. By the last week of May, a broader ridge develops across much of the country, leading to a warming trend. See the graphic below showing the dramatically different 500mb height anomalies for the beginning vs. end of May. So, we’ll have cooler temperatures in the first half of the month and warmer temperatures in the second half. This balances out to near-average temperatures for the month overall. Precipitation: Early Deficit, Limited Recovery The precipitation pattern is less well defined but follows a similar evolution. Early in the month, the same trough that brings cooler weather does not favor much rainfall (although there will be more rain to the south—in Asheville and Hickory—than to the north in Boone and SW VA). The precipitation signal for the final three weeks of May is somewhat wetter, but not dramatically so. We are not expecting intense or prolonged precipitation events. Rainfall during this time will come from weak fronts moving through the zonal flow and, by week four, from “hit-or-miss” afternoon and nighttime thundershowers. The net effect is slightly drier-than-average conditions for the month. May Outlook at a Glance
Week Temperature Precipitation
Week 1 (May 1–7) Cooler than average Drier than average
Week 2 (May 8–14) Cooler than average Near average
Week 3 (May 15–21) Trending warmer Near average
Week 4 (May 22–31) Above average Near average
Overall for May Below average Slightly below average
Factors Considered in this Forecast • The AO, NAO, and PNA indices strongly indicate the cooler start in the first week to 10 days of May, followed by a return to near-normal temperatures around mid-month or slightly later. AO (Arctic Oscillation) and NAO (North Atlantic Oscillation): Both indices take a sharp dip in the first 10 days of May, then gradually trend back toward neutral. PNA (Pacific–North American): Strongly positive early in the month, then trending toward neutral. • ENSO: For context, La Niña is gone. The central Pacific is now officially ENSO-neutral but trending warmer. There is a reasonable chance we are looking at a budding El Niño late in the month. However, May’s weather has little direct correlation with the El Niño–Southern Oscillation. That said, El Niño appears to be developing and could carry into next winter—that will be interesting. A trend toward El Niño also usually suppresses tropical activity in the Atlantic as we move into hurricane season. • Extended ECMWF, GFS, and Canadian modeling: All three models are in good agreement with the overall pattern for the month but differ somewhat on the timing and intensity of the temperature rebound in the last two weeks. The Canadian is slightly warmer and drier than the other two models. Climatological Average for May
